data.title: Lithium Americas in Loan Talks With Trump Administration, General Motors — WSJ
data.description:
By Scott Patterson and Christopher Otts

The Trump administration is in talks to take another stake in a company that wants a loan from the U.S. government.

Lithium Americas, a Vancouver-based mining company, is in negotiations with the U.S. Energy Department and automaker General Motors over the terms of its $2.26 billion government loan for a large lithium mining project in Nevada.

Talks concern what requirements must be met before the financing will be released, details of the loan itself, as well as new requests from the Energy Department that could add conditions or alter its terms, the company said. GM, a partner in the lithium project, is participating in the discussions.

The company's shares doubled in Wednesday trading to just under $6. Whether a deal will actually be reached remains unclear.

Lithium, a critical mineral used in electric-vehicle batteries, smartphones and renewable energy storage, has become a priority for the U.S. as it seeks to cut reliance on foreign suppliers.

U.S. officials are concerned about the viability of the project and wary that funds would be invested in a volatile industry dominated by Chinese players, said people briefed on the matter. Given China's outsize influence in lithium, some officials worry the country could manipulate the price to artificial lows, threatening the business model for the project.

The Trump administration has been reviewing loans for other clean-energy projects that were planned under President Joe Biden's administration.

Discussions have included the U.S. taking a stake in Lithium Americas as a condition of receiving a loan and GM, which has the option to buy all of the project's initial production, committing to a "take or pay" agreement for the project's output. Such a deal would require the automaker to either pay for lithium produced by the mining company or pay a penalty, people briefed on the matter said. GM declined to change its commitments amid an uncertain environment for electric vehicles, according to people familiar with the matter.

The automaker has invested $945 million so far to help bring the lithium mine online. The latest installment of $625 million was paid in December based on the Biden administration's commitment to support the project with federal money.

The clock is ticking. Lithium Americas is experiencing a cash crunch and needs the government funds to keep operating, according to a person familiar with the matter. If it is unable to reach a deal with the Trump administration in the coming days, it might have to mothball its Nevada mining project, which employs about 600 contractors, the person said.

Lithium Americas was awarded the loan under the Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing program to help build out the processing facilities at Thacker Pass, which is among the largest known lithium deposits in the U.S.

But the project has come under scrutiny. Earlier this month, an Energy Department official questioned its viability and administration officials have raised concerns about the company's ability to repay the loan given low lithium prices.

Write to Scott Patterson at scott.patterson@wsj.com and Christopher Otts at christopher.otts@wsj.com

data.title: Miners, defence stocks lifts FTSE 100, investors assess Fed Chair's remarks
data.description:
Key points:
  • FTSE 100 up 0.3%, FTSE 250 down 0.02%
  • Industrial metal miners jump on copper gains
  • Defence sector gains after Trump's comments on Ukraine
  • JD Sports Fashion reports 13.5% fall in first-half profit

Britain's FTSE 100 closed higher on Wednesday, led by miners and defence stocks, while investors assessed corporate updates and measured comments by Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

The benchmark FTSE 100 CURRENCYCOM:UK100 reversed earlier losses to gain 0.3%.

An index of industrial metal miners (.FTNMX551020) rose 3% after copper prices on the London Metal Exchange jumped to their highest level in over 15 months.

Antofagasta gained the most in the FTSE 100 with a 9.3% rise, while Glencore added nearly 3%.

Miner Anglo American rose 4.7% after Angola's state diamond firm Endiama bid for a minority stake in its diamond unit - De Beers.

The aerospace and defence (.FTNMX502010) sector rose 1.3%, mirroring gains in European peers after U.S. President Donald Trump, in an abrupt rhetorical shift, said he believed Ukraine could retake all of its territory now occupied by Russia.

Babcock International Group was among the top FTSE gainers, up 4.3%, while BAE Systems rose 2.2%.

Wall Street's main indexes were subdued in choppy trading on the day.

Traders were digesting comments made on Tuesday by Powell, who said the central bank needed to continue balancing the competing risks of high inflation and a weakening job market in its coming interest rate decisions — offering little clarity on the future path of rates.

Still, investors expect the U.S. central bank to cut interest rates in its remaining two meetings of the year.

Among individual stocks, Britain's JD Sports Fashion reported a 13.5% fall in first-half profit. Shares of the British sportswear retailer were down 0.8%.

The domestically focused FTSE 250 closed down 0.02%.

Pinewood Technologies slipped the most in the FTSE 250, down 14.9%, after the British auto retailer projected its 2025 EBITDA below analysts' consensus, according to Berenberg.

Baltic Classifieds Group's fell 5.1%, after the company reported results below expectations.

Conversely, Goodwin jumped 19.7% in the FTSE 250, after the mechanical engineering firm announced that its unit - Goodwin Steel Castings, had entered a strategic collaboration with Northrop Grumman International Trading.

data.title: US new home sales jump to more than 3-1/2-year high; economists dismiss rise as a fluke
data.description:
Key points:
  • New home sales jump 20.5% in August; July sales pace revised up
  • New housing inventory drops to eight-month low
  • Median new house price increases 1.9% to $413,500

By Lucia Mutikani

Sales of new U.S. single-family homes surged to the highest level in more than 3-1/2 years in August, but that likely exaggerates the housing market's health, and a weakening labor market could limit the boost from falling mortgage rates.

The bigger-than-expected increase in sales last month reported by the Commerce Department on Wednesday was shrugged off by economists, who noted that new housing data was extremely volatile and subject to revisions. They also said the jump in sales was at odds with subdued homebuilder sentiment.

"There is no obvious driver. I expect that this spike in sales will be largely reversed in coming months," said Stephen Stanley, chief U.S. economist at Santander U.S. Capital Markets.

"One could potentially point to lower mortgage rates ... but the bigger fall has come in September. One might think that builders capitulated and cut their asking prices sharply, but the average price of new homes sold in August actually jumped versus July."

New home sales shot up 20.5% to a seasonally adjusted annualized rate of 800,000 units last month, the highest level since January 2022, the Commerce Department's Census Bureau said. The increase was the biggest since August 2022.

The sales pace for July was revised higher to a rate of 664,000 units from the previously reported pace of 652,000 units. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast new home sales, which make up about 14% of U.S. home sales, easing to a rate of 650,000 units. June's sales pace was also upgraded.

New home sales, which are counted at the signing of a contract, are volatile on a month-to-month basis and subject to big revisions. They soared 15.4% on a year-over-year basis in August. Monthly sales rocketed 72.2% in the Northeast, which accounts for a small share of new housing construction.

They jumped 24.7% in the densely populated South and raced 12.7% in the Midwest. Sales rose 5.6% in the West.

The housing market has been in a slump because of higher mortgage rates, with residential investment contracting in the first half of this year.

MORTGAGE RATES ARE FALLING

Mortgage rates declined as the Federal Reserve prepared to resume easing monetary policy. The U.S. central bank cut its benchmark overnight interest rate last week by 25 basis points to the 4.00%-4.25% target. The Fed projected a steady pace of reductions for the rest of 2025.

The rate on the popular 30-year mortgage dropped to an 11-month low of 6.26% last week, data from mortgage finance agency Freddie Mac showed. It has been edging lower since mid-July and is down from around 7.04% in mid-January.

But the labor market has softened, with nonfarm payrolls gains averaging only 29,000 jobs per month in the three months to August compared to 82,000 during the same period last year.

"Lower mortgage rates should provide some lift to new home sales activity this fall," said Ben Ayers, a senior economist at Nationwide. "But builders are preparing for softer sales activity over the next six months with fewer single-family home projects breaking ground and fewer permits authorized."

Data from the Mortgage Bankers Association on Wednesday suggested homeowners were taking advantage of declining borrowing costs to refinance. There was a modest increase in applications for loans to buy a home last week.

With sales rising over the past three months, new housing inventory dropped to 490,000 units, the lowest level since last December, from 497,000 in July. Supply had hovered at levels last seen in late 2007. Builders have been cutting prices and offering incentives to reduce inventory. The median new house price increased 1.9% to $413,500 in August from a year earlier.

Most of the homes sold last month cost less than $500,000.

At August's sales pace, it would take 7.4 months to clear the supply of new houses on the market, down from 9.0 months in July. Homes under construction accounted for the bulk of the inventory last month.

"If, as seems likely, new sales drop back again, then homebuilders likely will respond to this overhang by trimming prices," said Oliver Allen, senior economist at

Pantheon Macroeconomics. "Another way in which homebuilders will try to clear inventory will be an even further pullback in new single-family construction projects in the coming months."

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1014 ET - Anthropic will drive significant revenue growth for Amazon Web Services, say Wells Fargo analysts, who upgrade Amazon to overweight from equal weight. The analysts increase their AWS revenue estimates by 3% for 2026 and 7% for 2027, largely due to higher expectations for revenue from Amazon and Anthropic's Project Rainier compute build-out. Meanwhile, market share losses for AWS will likely improve in 2026 and beyond as AWS accelerates, alleviating competitive concerns, the analysts say. Amazon is up 0.7% in early trading. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0820 ET - French telecommunications companies might consider market consolidation, Morgan Stanley analysts write in a note. France remains among Europe's most competitive telecom markets, they say. However, the country's major players could pursue mergers or acquisitions, but for different reasons. Altice might be motivated by potential synergies and the opportunity to reverse previous debt write-downs. Bouygues could seek consolidation to achieve greater scale. Meanwhile, Iliad may aim to acquire assets to strengthen its service offering, and Orange would benefit from reduced competition to support growth. Still, a successful deal would require several factors to align simultaneously--particularly on pricing and asset distribution--making any transaction risky, they say. Orange shares are down 0.7%, and Bouygues shares are up 0.05%. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0804 ET - Through its $100 billion investment in OpenAI, Nvidia is protecting its leadership in the chip market by securing demand from its largest customer, says Mizuho's Vijay Rakesh. The chips Nvidia will provide as part of the deal will be used by OpenAI for large-language model training, which cements Nvidia's dominance in AI training, Rakesh says "NVDA gained $150-200B in market cap after the announcement with, we believe, investors valuing its LLM training leadership," the analyst says. Some investors have expressed concern about the deal structure, but several other vendor-backed financing deals in the industry are all being driven by strong AI demand growth, the analyst says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0756 ET - Telecom Italia's latest bond issuance confirms the company is on a clear path toward financial improvement, Equita analyst Domenico Ghilotti writes in a note. The Italian telecommunications company issued a 500 million-euro bond at a 3.625% coupon, a move the analyst sees as further evidence of the company's strengthening finances. Additionally, the reduction in the average cost of debt is set to be a significant driver of enhanced free cash flow in the coming years, he says. Shares are flat at 0.43 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0742 ET - Business leaders in Canada are calling for artificial intelligence policy, regulation and incentives to be implemented, research by KPMG shows. A poll of 750 business leaders in late August and early September found that 92% want the federal government to set up a regulatory framework as soon as possible. And 89% reckon tax credits for small and medium-sized businesses to adopt AI would be a good start, while 91% say government must offer better incentives for workers to develop intellectual property and remain in the country. The poll also showed 88% believe Canada's new minister of AI will help Canada advance its position in AI and make the country more competitive, KPMG says. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0729 ET - Telecom Italia's stock price fails to reflect the potential for European market consolidation, particularly in the Italian market, Equita's Domenico Ghilotti writes in a note. The analyst sees consolidation as one of the most significant opportunities for the company, yet still largely unpriced by the market. This is also due to regulatory uncertainties and negotiation complexities, he adds. The company's chief executive, Pietro Labriola, recently reiterated the need for a regulatory environment more conducive to consolidation to support investment, he notes. Shares are up 0.2% at 0.43 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0654 ET - ASM International's long-term guidance reinforces its position as an industry outperformer for many years to come, ING analyst Marc Hesselink writes in a note. Despite a near-term setback, ING expects 2027 estimates to remain largely unchanged. The Dutch supplier of semiconductor-making equipment revised its full-year revenue forecast downward, citing weaker-than-expected demand in the fourth quarter. However, visibility into ASM's outperformance remains strong, supported by technology developments, Hesselink says. Shares are up 1.7% at 508.60 euros. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0521 ET - China's AI supply chain is building up a "self-contained virtuous cycle" from chips to AI models and applications, Morningstar analyst Phelix Lee says in an email. Both Alibaba's plan to expand capital spending on AI today and Huawei's unveiling of its AI chips roadmap last week signal stronger confidence in China-made AI models. Their continuous innovation and spending support demand for semiconductors, benefiting both foundries like SMIC, Hua Hong as well as other upstream equipment plays, he says. Better model performance leads to more AI deployment and further translates to increased investment in research and design for better chips, he adds. Alibaba and SMIC's shares ended 9.2% and 5.7% higher, respectively, in Hong Kong. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0406 ET - Tencent's gaming business remained solid in August, and CGS International analysts Lei Yang and Charlotte Zhou project 3Q gaming revenue to rise 18% on year. The company's gaming revenue growth is driven by evergreen titles such as "Honor of Kings" and new releases including "Delta Force." The latter debuted to strong popularity, and CGS expects daily active users to improve further with the launch of new gameplay and IP in 2H. The analysts also see significant commercial value in Tencent's licensing collaboration with Pop Mart and Kayou on toys and cards. CGS maintains an add rating on Tencent's stock, citing its mature gaming operating experience and robust new game pipeline, with a target price of HK$693.00. Tencent's shares are last at HK$647.50. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0344 ET - Com7 is set to benefit from a surge in iPhone 17 pre-sales, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Wasu Mattanapotchanart says in a research report. The Thai IT retail chain operator's iPhone 17 pre-sales grew by double-digits between September 12 and 21, prompting the brokerage to lift its 2025 core profit estimate for Com7 by 4%. Growth drivers for iPhone 17 include the base models being priced THB4,000 lower than iPhone 16 counterparts with same storage capacities. The brokerage also boosts its 2026-2027 core net profit estimates for Com7 by 5% to reflect the company's leasing of electric vehicle taxis. Maybank Securities (Thailand) raises the stock's target price to THB32.00 from THB30.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 2.0% higher at THB25.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0007 ET - Investors appear to be taking profit on Baidu following its recent share-price gains. The market had cheered the Chinese tech giant's AI chip developments, sending its Hong Kong-listed shares up 43% so far in September. Morningstar analyst Kai Wang says investors have likely turned more cautious, as the company's AI chips were actually released months ago and its key advertising business remains challenged. Nomura analysts note that the rise of AI search poses another threat to Baidu, whose search business has weakened significantly in recent years. The company's push into AI search has yet to arrest the decline in user numbers and time spent, they add in a note. Baidu's H shares are down 0.55% at midday after falling as much as 5.5% earlier. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2343 ET - LG Display is set to post a profit turnaround in 3Q,Shinhan Securities says in a note. The South Korean flat-screen maker is expected to report an operating profit of KRW458 billion for the July-September period, compared with a KRW116-billion loss in the previous quarter and a KRW81-billion loss a year earlier, Shinhan says. The anticipated turnaround is backed by the launches of new smartphones, tablet computers and smartwatches by Apple as well as seasonal demand and lowered depreciation costs for its organic light-emitting-diode business, Shinhan notes. The company stays on course to return to an annual operating profit in 2025 after three consecutive years of losses. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1942 ET - Telix Pharmaceuticals's revenue guidance of US$770 million-US$800 million looks achievable to UBS, especially after it secured transitional pass through, or TPT, status for its Gozellix imaging agent for prostate cancer in the U.S. Analyst David Dai suggests Telix could upgrade this guidance if Gozellix has a strong launch in 4Q 2025. "We believe TPT should give Gozellix significant reimbursement advantage over lower priced competitor F-18 PET agents (Pylarify and Posluma)," UBS says. "Additionally, Gozellix's longer shelf-life should also improve hospital experience, giving physicians more flexibility for scheduling." It expects Gozellix to gain significant market share in 4Q. Telix's shares rose 9.2% on Tuesday following the TPT approval by the U.S. Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1835 ET - Patient death rates rose while emergency-room staffing and pay levels fell at U.S. hospitals acquired in private-equity deals compared with others not held by PE firms, according to a study published Tuesday in the Annals of Internal Medicine. Deaths in PE-controlled ERs rose over 13% from a raw baseline, while salary expenses fell 18%, according to an abstract of the study. The federally funded project by researchers at Harvard University, the University of Chicago and the University of Pittsburgh, examined outcomes at 49 private-equity-backed institutions compared with 293 matched hospitals from 2009 to 2019. Researchers gathered data on more than 1 million patient visits and over 120,000 hospitalizations in intensive-care units at the PE hospitals and nearly 6.2 million ER visits and over 760,000 ICU stays in the matched institutions. (ted.bunker@wsj.com)

1435 ET - Kenvue is partially rebounding after sliding 7% Monday ahead of President Trump's broadside against Tylenol, Kenvue's medication with the active ingredient acetaminophen. Trump labeled Tylenol use in pregnant women as a potential cause of autism. In a report, BNP Paribas analyst Navann Ty says press releases from Health and Human Services and the FDA struck a "more measured and balanced" tone cautioning that a causal relationship has not been established and that doctors should consider minimizing the use of acetaminophen during pregnancy for low-grade fevers. Ty adds that she doesn't expect a near-term impact on Tylenol revenue but doesn't rule out a longer-term risk, with HHS gearing up for a nationwide public service campaign. Kenvue is up 2%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0815 ET - The market seems too optimistic on Commerzbank's stock, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note. Germany's fiscal stimulus plans have been driving investor enthusiasm and pushing shares higher, analysts write. Some of this is warranted given that earnings are expected to increase. "However, incorporating company targets...and these incremental gains, the stock currently trades at a 12% premium to the wider sector," they say, adding that they don't believe this is warranted. Consensus forecasts also appear optimistic and there is a risk that the M&A premium triggered by UniCredit's approach unravels over the next 12 months, they say. KBW downgrades the stock to underperform from market perform. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0726 ET - KBC Group is more likely to focus on M&A plans in line with its strategy, JPMorgan says in a research note. This would entail domestic deals or those in the CEE region, JPM writes after Bloomberg reported that the Belgian lender was in the early stages of evaluating a potential deal for Dutch peer ABN Amro. "While never say never, strategically, we would assume that a potential transaction from KBC would likely be more focused on the domestic Belgian insurance market or the CEE region," the analysts write. KBC is interested in establishing a bancassurance model in the CEE region. KBC has set its sights on Belgian insurer Ethias, expressed interest in entering the Romanian market, and recently bought a bank in Slovakia. Capital deployment is one of the key debates around the stock, and management tends to announce a decision around full-year results, the analysts add.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0722 ET - The rise in ABN Amro's shares earlier this week on the back of a Bloomberg report that suggested that KBC Group was in the early stages of considering a takeover is a reminder that the Dutch bank can be an attractive target, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. "An offer for ABN might have been financially attractive for KBC, but it would have come as a surprise," analyst Anke Reingen writes. Potential expression of interest is far from an actual transaction, as recent examples in the European banking sector have shown. BNP Paribas and Deutsche Bank are seen as potential suitors given their products' capabilities, she adds. "We do not think both feel the pressure to act, especially now, but might react if they see an opportunity disappear." (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0655 ET - KBC Group has clear strategic M&A goals so the idea that it would seek a takeover of Dutch peer ABN Amro is surprising, ING says in a research note. Bloomberg reported that the Belgian lender was in the early stages of evaluating a potential deal earlier this week, sending ABN Amro's shares up around 10%. "We strongly believe that it is a very unlikely outcome," analyst Jason Kalamboussis writes. The bank has for years pursued a strategy to enter Central and Eastern European countries and also has state-owned insurer Ethias in its sights. There doesn't seem to be a real basis for an approach on ABN Amro, he adds, noting that KBC doesn't have experience in buying large assets. Such a move would also dilute its returns, while the compaby doesn't need to resort to a defensive move to protect is Benelux ambitions, Kalamboussis notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0623 ET - The increase of Italian tycoon Francesco Gaetano Caltagirone's stake in Italian bank Monte Dei Paschi reduces risk for the stock, Equita says in a research note. Caltagirone's holding company asked for ECB authorization to increase its stake in the lender above 10%, to 10.9% following Monte Dei Paschi's takeover of Mediobanca--in which the holding company also owned a stake. Caltagirone is now Monte dei Paschi's second largest shareholder, after Delfin. The move leaves the possiblity of a further stake increase open, analyst Andrea Lisi writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0534 ET - Asian banks are likely to remain attractive thanks to domestic demand, local-currency earnings and above-average dividend yields, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. While Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts could pressure net-interest margins for some banks, Asian rates may not fall as much as U.S. rates, they note. These companies' return on equity is likely sustainable, MS analysts say, adding that Indian and Japanese banks are likely to see gains, which could support capital returns. Morgan Stanley maintains its overweight rating on Asia financials. "Key to the call on Asian banks is their ability to maintain earnings alongside their willingness and ability to maintain capital repayments to shareholders," they say. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0511 ET - Nordea is one of the highest quality banks in Europe on a return-on-tangible-equity basis and offers superior returns, lower earnings volatility, and better free capital generation than many peers, Jefferies analyst Alexander Demetriou writes. The coming investor day is an opportunity for the Helsinki-based bank to demonstrate its Nordic scale advantage over peers, with a focus on growth opportunities and cost efficiency, he adds. "With a distribution yield of circa 10% per annum and offering high and resilient returns, the shares remain our only buy in the Nordic banks sector." Shares rise 0.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0422 ET - Singapore equities are likely to continue appealing to income-oriented investors due to their attractive yields, according to HSBC Global Investment Research analysts. The city-state's equity market is still outperforming its Southeast Asian peers after last year's strong performance. "Investors have used Singapore as a hiding space while sentiment elsewhere is weak," the analysts write in a report. Singapore banks, known for their high dividend yields of 5%-7%, are also working on improving shareholder returns through buybacks, they add. However, HSBC notes that the lenders face net interest margin pressure amid continuing rate cuts. It maintains an underweight rating on Singapore equities. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0223 ET - Bank Mandiri (Persero) is likely to see a drag in its 2025 net profit from a one-off operating expense, UOB Kay Hian's Posmarito Pakpahan says in a research report. Management expects the bank's operating expenditures to rise by 20%-25% this year due to the one-off operating expense, which accounts for 10%-12% of its full-year operating expenditures. The brokerage cuts its 2025 net-profit estimate for the bank by 10% and lowers the stock's target price to IDR5,150.00 from IDR6,000.00, with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 0.2% lower at IDR4,400.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0203 ET - Revolut has work to do to grow and justify its latest valuation of $75 billion or more, Mergermarket's William Cain says. The U.K. fintech's ambition to amass 100 million customers in the mid-term--up from 65 million currently--underpins its valuation, he says. "Management's key task, if it is to win investor backing for an IPO, is to set out a clear roadmap of how it intends to reach 100 million customers and the extent to which this can be monetised," he adds. The group is increasingly in the radar for investors for an initial public offering, following the solid pricing achieved by Klarna when it floated in New York earlier this month. Revolut plans to invest $13 billion in the next few years to support its growth plans. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0154 ET - Revolut's recent milestones and investment plans are a huge vote of confidence in the U.K. and Europe, says Martin Mignot, a partner at Index Ventures, one of the U.K. fintech's largest shareholders. This "sends a clear message: we're moving to a point where European companies with $50 billion valuations and thousands of employees are quickly becoming an achievable goal, rather than a far-fetched aspiration," he writes on LinkedIn. The U.K. fintech is planning to invest $13 billion to expand globally, it aims to grow its retail customer base to 100 million in 2027 from 65 million currently and plans to enter 30 new markets by 2030. Mignot adds that fixing obstacles such as Europe's bureaucracy and fragmentation "will be critical if we want not one, but a hundred Revoluts to emerge." (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

2005 ET - Platinum Asset Management's merger with L1 isn't enough to turn UBS analysts bullish on the stock. With shareholder approval for the transaction secured, the UBS analysts incorporate L1's earnings into their outlook for the Australian wealth manager, driving EPS forecast increases of 11% for fiscal 2026, 16% for fiscal 2027, and 61% for fiscal 2028. They tell clients in a note that L1 should achieve strong organic earnings growth, and forecast A$12.5 million in fiscal 2026 cost reductions across the whole group. UBS lifts its target price 49% to A$0.79 but stays neutral on the stock. Shares are up 1.4% at A$0.735. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0948 ET - Oil futures are higher on continued unease about Russian supplies, while the market looks to U.S. inventory data from the EIA. The API yesterday reported a 3.8 million decline in crude stocks, analysts note. Analysts in a WSJ survey predict a 300,000 barrel drop in crude inventories following the previous week's unexpectedly large 9.3 million barrel draw. EIA data are scheduled for release at 10:30 a.m. ET. WTI is up 1.5% at $64.37 a barrel, and Brent rises 1.4% to $68.59. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0446 ET - Seatrium's planned sale of its U.S. shipbuilding facility in Texas is likely a good move, given the labor shortage and cost challenges that it's faced in recent years, say its bulls at CGS International. The S$65 million sale is likely to result in gains of S$26 million, progressively recognized, say analysts Lim Siew Khee and Meghana Kande in a note. The offshore and marine company is planning to deliver two legacy projects by end-2025, which could allow it to trim provisions for onerous contracts and reduce operating expenses by S$20 million next year. CGS International maintains an add rating and S$2.80 target price, noting that Seatrium is on track for core-profit turnaround. Shares are 0.8% lower at S$2.41. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2223 ET - Petronas Dagangan's sales outlook could weaken, with Malaysia set to introduce a 300-liter monthly limit for subsidized RON95 fuel for citizens at the end of September, CGS International's Raymond Yap says in a note. While most Malaysians are unlikely to exceed the quota, identity verification may reduce unauthorized purchases by foreigners and lower overall volumes, the analyst writes. The government's crackdowns on unauthorized purchases of subsidized diesel have already pressured the gas-station operator's volumes, with sales falling 2% on year in 1Q and 1% in 2Q, he notes. CGS International maintains its reduce rating and target price of MYR16.74 on the stock. Shares are 1.1% higher at MYR22.24. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1953 ET - Amplitude Energy's decision to expand its East Coast Supply Project in southeastern Australia gets a tick from Jarden. Amplitude is raising A$150 million to support that effort. Analyst Nik Burns likes the inclusion of a fourth well in the ECSP, specifically to target the Nestor deposit. "We are bullish on east coast gas market prices (given our forecast of declining gas supplies from 2028) and Nestor adds another potential gas supply option," Jarden says. The addition of Nestor also reduces the reliance on success at one or both of the first two wells in the ECSP program--Elanora and Isabella. Jarden retains a buy call on Amplitude. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1927 ET - With an acquisition by the XRG consortium off the table, investors in Santos must focus again on the Australian energy company's fundamentals. Citi finds lots to like. It forecasts solid production growth supported by the Barossa natural-gas project in Australia and Pikka Phase 1 project in the U.S. The startup of these new projects should see capex moderate. Santos is also likely to generate strong free cash flow, analyst Tom Wallington says. "On our numbers, investors are effectively paying for Base Business and Committed Growth while getting Speculative Growth (Papua LNG, Dorado, and Narrabri), for free," Citi says. It keeps a "buy" call on Santos, but lowers its price target by 14%, to A$7.50/share. Santos ended Tuesday at A$6.74. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1504 ET - Crude futures settle higher after four straight sessions of losses as Ukraine continues attacks on Russian oil facilities and President Trump tells the U.N. General Assembly he's prepared to use strong tariff measures to get Russia to end the war. Reports that Russia is considering additional restrictions on fuel exports contributed to gains. "The primary buying impetus is being provided by occasional spikes in geopolitical risk that spurred the bulk of today's purchases," Ritterbusch says in a note. WTI settles up 1.8% at $63.41 a barrel, and Brent rises 1.6% to $67.63. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1229 ET - OKLO is gaining ground on with its nuclear plans, but the valuation is still a little high, says Seaport's Jeff Campbell. In a report, the analyst says that the company's flagship microreactor design, the 75MW Aurora Powerhouse, is going to be built, and from it, the company will also generate data used to help validate performance, safety, and reliability. Shares have risen over 500% in 2025 alone, and about 1,600% over the past 52 weeks. "While we continue to see a number of positives in OKLO's business development, we are stepping to the sidelines for now based on the current stock valuation," Campbell says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1218 ET - A possible U.S. government stake in Lithium Americas would give its Thacker Pass project in Nevada a vote of confidence. The project, for which it is negotiating a $2.26 billion government loan, has come under scrutiny as Department of Energy officials questioned its viability and administration officials raised concerns about the company's ability to repay the loan given low lithium prices. TD Cowen analyst David Deckelbaum says in a report that "a government stake would lend credence to project completion and expansion to multiple phases with perhaps enhanced economics." Lithium Americas shares double in high volume trading. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1115 ET - A potential U.S. government stake in Lithium Americas would a clear indication that the critical material is key to the future. Dan Ives of Wedbush Securities tells the WSJ it would be "a shot across the bow" and mean "the U.S. government is planting a flag on the lithium supply front." Lithium Americas nearly doubles following a Reuters report that the U.S. government is negotiating to take up to a 10% stake in the Canadian miner. Ives says it would be "an aggressive and smart move in our view in this arms race for energy." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1104 ET - Lithium Americas is skyrocketing after the company says it's in talks with the U.S. Department of Energy and General Motors over terms of its $2.26 billion government loan for its large lithium project in Nevada. The news comes after Reuters reported the U.S. is considering taking up to a 10% equity stake in the company. Analysts at Jefferies say to the extent the US government believes equity stakes have a significantly lower political cost than tax increases, "mark-to-market equity stakes can offset deficit financing, higher deficit spending can support corporate profits, interest rates can trend lower to support equity valuations, and equity markets can view equity stakes as a leading indicator of favorable ROIC [return on invested capital]." Lithium Americas surges 98% to C$8.35.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0037 ET - Mineral Resources' US$700 million debt refinancing should give earnings a small boost and signals the potential for more savings ahead, says Morgan Stanley analyst Rahul Anand. The latest notes have a rate of 7.000%, compared with 8.125% on the notes it is refinancing. Anand expects the rate cut to lift FY 2026 and FY 2027 underlying EPS by 2.2% to A$0.93/share and 2.1% to A$2.06/share, respectively. "The note issue reinforces our view that MIN faced a debt optics issue; however, serviceability and refinancing remained manageable," he says. Mineral Resources has another US$2.35 billion of notes, due between November 2027 and May 2030, on which it could also save cash by refinancing, Anand says. MS has an overweight rating and a A$41.50 target on the stock. It is up 3.9% at A$40.32. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2304 ET - Silver futures could rally to $46/oz, or even higher, by 2Q 2026, according to Commonwealth Bank of Australia analyst Vivek Dhar. In a note, he says risks are "tilted to the upside given the downside risks to the U.S. dollar and stronger structural safe-haven demand for silver." Silver prices are at a 14-year high, driven by haven demand and a strong inverse relationship with the U.S. dollar, Dhar says. Spot silver, up roughly 50% year to date, is down 0.3% in Asia at $43.88/oz. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2129 ET - Press Metal Aluminium is expected to deliver steady 2H earnings, supported by firmer aluminum prices, effective hedging and easing input costs, Hong Leong IB analyst Thye May Ting says in a note. Around 50%-60% of 2025 sales volume are already hedged at slightly higher prices of $2,600/ton, helping offset weaker Main Japanese Port aluminum premium, she says. Margins should be supported by lower carbon anode prices and a stable aluminium-alumina cost ratio, she adds. Thye says the company's ongoing alumina refinery expansion is also positive, as it could eventually meet up to 55% of its annual alumina requirements. Hong Leong maintains a buy rating on Press Metal and keeps its target price at MYR6.60. Shares are 0.7% lower at MYR5.70. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1300 ET - The surcharge for transporting aluminum to buyers in the U.S. is at an unprecedented 74-76 cents a pound, says commodity research firm Fastmarkets. But there may be more upside left for the premium--referred to as the "midwest premium"--before it fully reflects the effect of the 50% duties on aluminum imports into the U.S., says Andy Farida of Fastmarkets. Aluminum stockpiles in the U.S. are dwindling, he says, and the cost for businesses to get the metal they need is only rising. "'High replacement cost' has been the main go to word among market participants who need material on the spot," says Farida. The premium may hit 85 cents a pound this year, he adds, potentially going to $1 a pound if a major economic event emerges. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1558 GMT - General Motors' North America segment looks likely to drive margins back to a target range of 8% to 10% as early as next year, even as added tariff costs remain a hurdle, UBS analysts say in a note. GM should benefit from lower tariff rates on imports from South Korea and potentially Mexico, as well as lower U.S. emissions standards, which would mean the company won't have to expense regulatory credits and can shift its portfolio to higher-profit vehicles, the analysts say. GM should also get a lift from its continued cost-cutting efforts and potential price hikes, helped by further U.S. rate cuts, they say. They upgrade the stock to buy from neutral and raise their price target to $81 from $56. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1328 GMT - AutoZone finished slightly weaker Tuesday after an $80 million tariff-driven accounting charge caused it to miss F4Q earnings and margin expectations. But the company's strong overall fundamentals show investor concerns are overblown, say Oppenheimer analysts. "We look upon underlying trends at AZO as stronger than headline figures suggest and view yesterday's modest pullback in shares as inconsistent with seemingly clear signals of improving fundamental momentum," the analysts say. The company recorded strong F4Q comparable sales growth and management expects at least 3% ticket inflation for the remainder of the year, the analysts say, adding that AutoZone remains confident in its ability to manage inflation dynamics with pricing and other mitigation efforts. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1242 GMT - AutoZone's F4Q results showed it is continuing to perform significantly better than other auto part retailers, say D.A. Davidson analysts Michael Baker and Keegan Cox. The gap between AutoZone's domestic comparable sales growth and the industry's was 350 basis points during AutoZone's F4Q. "That gap widened from 100 bps last quarter and was the widest since 2Q21 as AZO continues to take share at an accelerating pace and out-comp competitors," the analysts say. The company's performance is being fueled by investments in its distribution infrastructure, which is allowing it to take share in the commercial business. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1036 GMT - Rheinmetall is set for a near-term revenue boost as the German government moves to convert existing framework contracts into firm orders, Mwb-research analyst Jens-Peter Rieck says. The German arms maker already sits on some of the largest framework agreements with the German defense ministry. If these become real contracts, then optional backlog transforms into near-term revenue, he notes. Russian probes into the North Atlantic Treaty Organization's airspace have increased pressure to get weapons delivered fast, Rieck adds. Governments will prioritize suppliers who can deliver at scale and on time, he says. Rheinmetall has already scaled assembly lines, staffed up and secured call offs, while many competitors have lagged behind in building new capacity, Rieck says. Mwb-research confirms its price target for the stock at 2,500 euros. Shares are up 1.3% at 1,936.50 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0817 GMT - Volkswagen has been conservative in its downgraded margin guidance, which is understandable given the uncertainty of EU-U.S. tariffs and the lower outlook at subsidiary Traton, HSBC analysts write. The company announced a 5.1 billion-euro operating profit hit from issues at Porsche, but its downgrade to margin guidance represents a 4.9 billion-8.1 billion-euro operating profit hit based on HSBC estimates. Despite the issues at Porsche, the narrative at Volkswagen is largely intact, HSBC adds. "Strong orders and the start of savings should underpin growth." Around 2 billion euros of annual savings from new labor agreements should arrive in the short term. Fixed-cost savings of 200 million euros in the second quarter show overhead is starting to come down, with more to come. Shares fall 1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0721 GMT - European defense stocks surged in opening trade, leading the Stoxx 600 list of risers, after U.S. President Trump said Tuesday evening that Kyiv could win back "all of Ukraine" and said Russia looks like be a "paper tiger." Saab and Hensoldt surge 5.3% and 4.7% respectively. Renk Group is up 3.1%, Leonardo rises 2.5%, and Thales climbs 2.4%. Meanwhile, Rheinmetall, Dassault Aviation, QinetiQ and BAE Systems all advance 1.8%. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0718 GMT - NIO's strong orders and better sales across China's battery electric vehicle sector should benefit the automaker's shares, Citi analysts say in a note. They expect production to ramp up, estimating October's total production capacity and delivery to be 47,000-49,000 units and to exceed 50,000 in November. Citi raises its 2026 and 2027 sales forecasts to 500,000 and 571,00 units, respectively, while projecting higher gross profit margins at 16.8% in 2026 and 18.1% in 2027. Citi opens a 30-day upside catalyst watch on the automaker, raising its target on NIO's ADRs to US$8.60 from US$8.10 and on its Hong Kong shares to HK$65.90 from HK$62.50. The bank maintains a buy rating. NIO's ADRs closed at US$6.93, while the Hong Kong stock gained 3.1% to HK$55.40. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0447 GMT - Grab is likely to benefit from the adoption of self-driving cars in Singapore, but not anytime soon, Maybank Research analyst Hussaini Saifee writes in a note. Autonomous vehicle costs will likely fall with tech deflation, as compared to driver costs which are set to rise, assuming an annual inflation of 4%, Saifee says. Assuming that about 20% of Grab's fleet transitions to self-driving cars, by 2030, it could translate to about $71 million in annual savings, Saifee says. Given its early partnerships with AV firms like WeRide and its operational strength, Grab is well-placed to be both a platform and fleet operator of AVs, while its strong balance sheet supports infrastructure investment and mitigates downside risk, Saifee adds. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0324 GMT - China's durable goods sales, mainly home appliances and autos, could become a drag on economic growth in 4Q. Both segments have been key beneficiaries of Beijing's trade-in program which is set to end this year. Nomura analysts forecast home appliances sales in 4Q will shrink 20.0% on year after an expected increase of 14.2% in 3Q, citing planned production cuts for most of 2H and assuming no further government subsidies this year. Auto sales have been slowing in recent months but year-end sales could be supported by orders before the reinstatement of purchase taxes on EVs from 2026. The analysts predict passenger car sales growth by volume in 4Q will drop to 0.0% from 10.8% in 1H. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

2333 GMT - Transurban's bull at Citi is excited by the toll road owner's A$10 billion pipeline of opportunities. Citi says the pipeline includes 25% stakes in two new toll road developments in the U.S. states of Georgia and Tennessee in partnership with Ferrovial. There are opportunities on Transurban's existing network in Brisbane--namely, Logan Widening and Gateway Widening. There's also potential to make the I-95 toll road in the U.S. bi-directional, Citi says. "While these new development may take 5+ years to contribute to free cash, we believe winning these projects could highlight solid medium-term growth pipeline for Transurbuan," analyst Suraj Nebhani says. Citi has a A$16.10/share price target on Transurban, which ended Tuesday at A$14.09. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: APEX Entertainment Announces New Partnership with Syracuse Football Standouts Duce Chestnut & Dan Villari
data.description:
Top Orange Athletes Join Brand's Continued Support of Collegiate Athletes in the Syracuse Community

SYRACUSE, NY / ACCESS Newswire / September 24, 2025 / Apex Entertainment®, a premier family entertainment destination with a location at Destiny USA in Syracuse, announces a new NIL partnership with Syracuse University football players Duce Chestnut and Dan Villari, in collaboration with SU Football NIL. Chestnut and Villari become the latest collegiate athletes to team up with the brand, continuing its multi-year tradition of supporting standout Orange athletes in the region.

This partnership follows previous alliances with Syracuse football stars including Kyle McCord, Oronde Gadsden II, Garrett Shrader, and Sean Tucker, and reflects a longstanding commitment from Apex to uplift excellence in youth, sports, and community.

"We're excited to welcome Duce and Dan to the Apex family," said Rob Luzzi, Senior Director of Marketing at RAVentures. "They are game changers on the field and respected presences within the Syracuse community. They bring the kind of energy, drive, and local pride that make them perfect fits for Apex and its guests."

"I'm proud to be back at ‘Cuse and excited to partner with a brand like Apex that truly supports the community," said Chestnut. "It's great to be part of a fun place bringing people together - whether it's football fans, families, or students showing their Orange pride."

"Partnering with Apex is about representing my teammates, my coaches, and the larger Orange fan base," said Villari. "I've worked hard to grow in my role here at Syracuse, and I'm energized by the opportunity to give back to the community that's given me so much."

Chestnut, a dynamic defensive back, rejoined the Orange in 2025 after previously playing two standout seasons at Syracuse before transferring to LSU. Now back in orange and blue, he brings elite talent, experience, and leadership to the Syracuse defense. During his earlier seasons with the Orange, he earned Freshman All-American honors, was a two-time All-ACC selection, and became a fan favorite for his playmaking ability and lockdown coverage. Hailing from Camden, New Jersey, Duce is enrolled in the College of Arts and Sciences.

Villari, a versatile tight end and redshirt senior from Massapequa, New York, has made a name for himself through adaptability and multi-role contributions. After transferring from Michigan, he has appeared in numerous games for Syracuse, catching passes, rushing, and even taking snaps as a quarterback in various formations. Villari's recent performances - including being a primary target in many offensive schemes - underscore his growing leadership and value to the Orange.

About APEX Entertainment

Apex Entertainment® is a family entertainment destination for people of all ages. With four locations in Massachusetts, New York and Virginia, Apex Entertainment is the largest local space for indoor family fun with world class attractions all under one roof. Headquartered in Westford, Mass. and established in 2017, Apex Entertainment is part of RAVentures, which owns and operates hospitality brands and real estate and development companies. https://www.apexentertainment.com/.

data.title: DAX Ends Slightly Higher
data.description:
The DAX in Frankfurt turned slightly positive to close at 23,681.7 on Wednesday, advancing for a second day, mainly supported by defense stocks on Trump's Ukraine comments.

US President Donald Trump signaled a major policy shift on Tuesday, suggesting that Ukraine could reclaim all its territory from Russia.

Meanwhile, Fed Chair Jerome Powell reiterated caution over further easing, citing ongoing risks around inflation and labour market imbalances.

On the domestic front, IFO data revealed business sentiment in Germany dropped more than expected in September, casting a shadow over the economic recovery in Europe’s largest economy.

data.title: Freeport Shares Decline After Declaring Force Majeure Tied to Deadly Mud Rush
data.description:
By Emon Reiser

Shares for Freeport McMoRan declined after the miner notified investors of significant production delays tied to a fatal incident at its operations in Indonesia.

The Phoenix-based company's stock declined 11% to $40.18 in midday trading. Despite the intraday decline, shares for Freeport are up about 6% year to date.

Freeport on Wednesday said the bodies of two of its workers were found, and five of its workers remain missing after 800,000 tons of wet material flowed into its Grasberg mine Sept. 8. The company declared force majeure on contracted supplies from the mine, stoking scarcity concerns that pushed up copper prices globally. Force majeure releases the company of its contractual obligations due to the tragedy.

For the third quarter, Freeport expects consolidated sales to be 4% lower for copper and 6% lower for gold than its estimates in July. The company in July said its consolidated sales were expected to approximate 1 billion pounds of copper, 350,000 ounces of gold and 18 million pounds of molybdenum for the third quarter.

The company said its 2026 production could potentially be 35% lower than its estimates of 1.7 billion pounds of copper and 1.6 million ounces of gold prior to the incident.

Freeport's Grasberg mine is located in one of the world's largest copper and gold deposits in the world. The copper concentrate produced there is typically shipped to PT Smelting in Indonesia, Atlantic Copper in Spain and third-party smelters outside of Indonesia, according to the company's filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission. Freeport earlier this year warned investors that its business in Indonesia was slowing as it awaited repairs on a smelter at the mine that was damaged in a fire last year.

Write to Emon Reiser at emon.reiser@wsj.com

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1142 ET - Oil prices rise further in afternoon trade, with Brent crude topping $68 a barrel as traders weigh demand trends in the U.S. and Russian supply risks. The international oil benchmark is up 1.7% to $68.12 a barrel, while WTI rises 2.1% to $64.73 a barrel. The latest EIA report showed weekly U.S. crude stockpiles fell more than expected by 607,000 barrels, while gasoline inventories were down by 1.1 million barrels. Meanwhile, investors continue to monitor developments in the Russia-Ukraine war following President Trump's shift in tone at the United Nations. According to DNB, Russian seaborne crude exports on a four-week moving average basis are at a 16-month high, up 500,000 barrels a day on year following a series of Ukrainian attacks on Russian infrastructure. "The attacks on refineries are lowering refinery runs, and Russia is diverting more crude to exports," analysts at the firm say. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1120 ET - Copper prices surged as much as 3% in afternoon trade after Freeport-McMoRan declared force majeure on contracted supplies from its Grasberg mine in Indonesia, stoking concerns over global supplies. Futures on the London Metal Exchange are up 2.9% to $10,274 a metric ton, after reaching $10,341 earlier in the session. The Phoenix-based miner said a preliminary assessment following a fatal incident earlier in September pointed to significant production delays in the fourth quarter and into 2026, with a return to pre-incident operating levels potentially occurring in 2027. Production at its Indonesian subsidiary in 2026 could be around 35% lower than previously forecast. Freeport's third-quarter consolidated sales are now forecast to be approximately 4% lower for copper. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1115 ET - A potential U.S. government stake in Lithium Americas would a clear indication that the critical material is key to the future. Dan Ives of Wedbush Securities tells the WSJ it would be "a shot across the bow" and mean "the U.S. government is planting a flag on the lithium supply front." Lithium Americas nearly doubles following a Reuters report that the U.S. government is negotiating to take up to a 10% stake in the Canadian miner. Ives says it would be "an aggressive and smart move in our view in this arms race for energy." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1104 ET - Lithium Americas is skyrocketing after the company says it's in talks with the U.S. Department of Energy and General Motors over terms of its $2.26 billion government loan for its large lithium project in Nevada. The news comes after Reuters reported the U.S. is considering taking up to a 10% equity stake in the company. Analysts at Jefferies say to the extent the US government believes equity stakes have a significantly lower political cost than tax increases, "mark-to-market equity stakes can offset deficit financing, higher deficit spending can support corporate profits, interest rates can trend lower to support equity valuations, and equity markets can view equity stakes as a leading indicator of favorable ROIC [return on invested capital]." Lithium Americas surges 98% to C$8.35.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1030 ET - Gold prices are on track for their strongest annual rally since 1979 after breaking above the $3,800-a-troy-ounce mark, according to Saxo. Futures have surged nearly 44% this year, buoyed by U.S. interest rate cuts, central-bank buying and ETF inflows, as well as strong safe-haven demand due to persistent geopolitical uncertainties. "The speed of the move underscores how shallow any corrections have been in recent weeks and months as dips continue to be met with fresh buying interest, a pattern consistent with broader macro support rather than speculative froth alone," analysts at the firm say. Meanwhile, silver has also rallied, topping $44 a troy ounce and surging more than 53% year-to-date as it benefits from structural demand growth from photovoltaics and electrification. "Its dual identity as both a monetary and industrial metal has worked to its advantage," Saxo analysts say. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1022 ET - Live cattle futures are down 0.2% in early trading on the CME, pulling back from the near record level of $2.40/lb that most-active futures settled at yesterday. Volatility remains a daily factor for the market, says AgResource in a note. Boxed beef prices have been showing weakness, the firm says, while slaughter margins also drop. Lean hog futures are also down, shedding 1% in early trading. Hog traders will be closely watching the USDA's next Hogs and Pigs report, which is scheduled to be published at 3 p.m. eastern time Thursday. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1002 ET - The USDA has again reported new high-volume sales of U.S. agricultural exports this week, with Mexico again purchasing a large amount of U.S. corn. The USDA says that 312,956 metric tons of corn were sold to Mexico for delivery in the 2025/26 marketing year. Additionally, 101,400 tons of soybean meal were sold to Guatemala, also for delivery in 2025/26. Mexico has been a frequent buyer of large lots of U.S. corn over the past week, showing up in at least three USDA notices since last Wednesday. The increased pace of buying comes as CBOT corn futures climb back from a multi-year low found in mid-August. The most-active corn contract is down 0.4% in early trading. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0956 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are holding their ground with a pickup in LNG feedgas limiting some early selling pressure. The start of production at the third unit of Cheniere's Corpus Christi facility last week is helping to lift LNG demand back above 15 Bcf a day despite the maintenance outage at Cove Point, "so overall supportive to late-year demands being well over 16 Bcf/d and even higher once we see gas begin to flow into Golden Pass," Tradition Energy's Gary Cunningham says in a note. The Nymex front month is up 0.2% at $2.858/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0948 ET - Oil futures are higher on continued unease about Russian supplies, while the market looks to U.S. inventory data from the EIA. The API yesterday reported a 3.8 million decline in crude stocks, analysts note. Analysts in a WSJ survey predict a 300,000 barrel drop in crude inventories following the previous week's unexpectedly large 9.3 million barrel draw. EIA data are scheduled for release at 10:30 a.m. ET. WTI is up 1.5% at $64.37 a barrel, and Brent rises 1.4% to $68.59. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0943 ET - Excluding the U.S. dollar, CAD remains the worst-performing major currency in 2025, and is likely to remain in a slump for the remainder of the year, says Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at forex firm Corpay. Traders are souring on CAD because the Trump administration's tariff policy have exposed Canadian economy's structural weaknesses--like miserable productivity, high household debt and housing-market vulnerabilities. "We think this dynamic will remain in place through year end as growth remains moribund," Schamotta says, adding the Bank of Canada is likely to cut its policy rate at least one more time. Schamotta believes there might be room for recovery early next year, depending on new pro-growth policies from PM Mark Carney and possible trade resolution with Washington. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0909 ET - More soybeans from Argentina are going to China after Argentina's government cut export taxes on grains. "China had bought 10-12 cargoes of soybeans," says John Stewart & Associates in a note. "That number has increased to nearly 20 cargoes, equating to roughly 1.3 million metric tons of soybeans." It signals China is opting to choose South American alternatives rather than buy U.S. soybean exports, continuing a hands-off approach as trade negotiations between China and the U.S. continue. CBOT soybeans are up 0.5% pre-market, while corn rises 0.2% and wheat is up 0.5%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0902 ET - A halt to EU imports of Russian LNG is unlikely to have a significant impact on global gas markets as Moscow's supplies would be redirected elsewhere, according to Goldman Sachs. In its latest package of sanctions against the Kremlin, the European Commission said it proposed to ban purchases of LNG at the end of 2026, a year earlier than initially planned. "This halt in imports, if implemented, would have limited impact on global gas balances and European energy prices given it would change the allocation, though not the total availability, of global LNG supplies," analysts at the U.S. bank say. Russian LNG currently accounts for nearly 20% of the EU's total LNG imports. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: FTSE MIB Slips Amid Fed Uncertainty
data.description:
The FTSE MIB slipped to 42,474 on Wednesday as investors digested cautious comments from Fed Chair Jerome Powell on the timing of further US rate cuts and followed domestic merger headlines.

Leonardo jumped 4.5% after hawkish NATO remarks from US President Trump boosted defense names.

Banco BPM gained 1.2% as the Italian government said it would set conditions on a potential tie-up with Crédit Agricole, while UniCredit added 0.3% amid ongoing takeover speculation with Commerzbank.

In contrast, Monte dei Paschi lost 1.9%, the bank was listed as a pontential M&A option by Banco BPM.

Notes:

1.

Rule 8 of the Takeover Code (the "Code") sets out the circumstances in which Dealing Disclosures and/or Opening Position Disclosures are required to be made by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them, persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more and exempt principal traders ("EPTs") connected with the parties to an offer.

2.

Where an offeror is marked "Disclosure of dealings and positions in this offeror is not required", dealings and positions in relevant securities of that offeror are not required to be disclosed under Rule 8. This is either because it has been announced that the offer or possible offer is, or is likely to be, solely in cash or because disclosure is not required for some other reason.

3.

Dealings and positions required to be disclosed under Rule 8 of the Code include dealings and positions in derivatives referenced to, and options in respect of, relevant securities of an offeree company and, where appropriate, an offeror (see Note 2 above).

4.

The information set out in the Disclosure Table relating to the relevant securities in issue of an offeree company or of an offeror (and any amendments thereto) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by such company under Rule 2.9 of the Code. Similarly, details of any deadline set under Rule 2.6 by which a potential offeror must announce either a firm intention to make a bid or that it does not intend to make an offer (or, in the case of Rule 2.6(a), by which the Panel must consent to an extension of the deadline) has been extracted from the relevant announcement made by the offeree company.

5.

Additions and deletions of offeree companies and offerors are reflected in the Disclosure Table throughout the day, following the relevant announcement. Other amendments may also be made throughout the day. In any event, details of the classes of relevant securities issued by an offeree or offeror company, the number of such securities in issue ("NSI") and the International Securities Identification Number ("ISIN") of such securities announced under Rule 2.9 of the Code before 5.00 pm each day will be included in the final version of the Disclosure Table by 5.30 pm that day. The final version of the Disclosure Table issued each day will be marked "Final" and persons required to make Dealing Disclosures and Opening Position Disclosures are advised to use that final version. Any changes to the Rule 2.6 deadline of a potential offeror will also be reflected in the "Final" version of the Disclosure Table, although any such changes will not be shown in the "Amendments" section at the top of the Disclosure Table.

6.

Dealing Disclosures by the parties to an offer, persons acting in concert with them and connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the business day (as defined in the Code) following the date of the relevant dealing and Dealing Disclosures by persons with an interest in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by 3.30 pm on the business day following the date of the relevant dealing.

7.

Opening Position Disclosures by the parties to an offer and by connected EPTs must be made by no later than 12 noon on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Where required, Opening Position Disclosures by persons with interests in relevant securities of 1% or more must be made by no later than 3.30 pm on the 10th business day after the commencement of the offer period or the announcement that first identifies an offeror (as the case may be). Details of the time and date at which an offer period commenced and at which an offeror was first identified are set out in the Disclosure Table. In order to calculate the deadline for Opening Position Disclosures, it is therefore necessary to count forward 10 business days from the relevant date.

8.

Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service ("RIS").

9.

This Note is applicable if it says "See Note 9 below" by the name of the company in the Disclosure Table above.  The Executive has granted the company a dispensation from the requirements under the Code that announcements must be published via a RIS. The company is instead required to publish all announcements on its website. The Executive has also granted a dispensation from Note 3 on Rule 8 of the Code that disclosures made under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a RIS. Therefore, Opening Position Disclosures and Dealing Disclosures required under Rule 8 of the Code may be made to the company at the address stated in the announcement that commenced the offer period.
